blurb: Once or twice a year, Atlanta shuts down streets along the Beltline and turns them into a giant open park for biking, walking, rollerblading, and just wandering. No cars, no noise, just people actually using the city the way it was meant to be used. May 31st is the first one of the year, which makes it the one with the most pent-up energy. It is a great excuse to get on the Beltline if you have been putting it off, or to do it in a way that feels more like a festival than a workout. Free, fun, and genuinely unlike anything else on the calendar.
scoring_notes: Streets Alive only happens a handful of times a year, giving the May 31 date real first-of-the-season energy. Good fit for active, educated East Cobb residents who enjoy the Beltline. The Beltline is a 25-30 minute drive from East Cobb, which slightly limits audience match, but the event's scale and novelty make it worth the trip for many readers.

blurb: The Georgia Renaissance Festival is in its final weekends, and if you have never been, this is the push you needed. It is a full-scale time-travel experience: jousting, costumes, period food, artisan vendors, and performances running all day across a massive fairground. It draws a crowd every year for a reason. The drive to Fairburn is about 45 minutes, so make a day of it. Kids love it, adults love it, and anyone who goes in thinking it is cheesy usually comes out a convert. Last chance this season ends May 31.
scoring_notes: The closing-weekend angle adds genuine urgency. Strong fit for families with teens who want an experiential outing, and adults who enjoy themed events. The drive is a trade-off but the scale of the event justifies it. Annual but the season-ending window is a real now-or-wait-a-year hook.

date: Saturday (specific date not confirmed in source)
blurb: 46-foot-long ornately decorated boats, racing across Lake Allatoona to the beat of a live drummer on board each one. The Acworth-Cobb Dragon Boat Festival is rooted in ancient Chinese tradition and has grown into one of the more visually spectacular free events in metro Atlanta. You do not need to know anything about dragon boat racing to enjoy it. You just need to show up. The event also supports local cancer patients and survivors, so there is real heart behind the spectacle. Worth the drive out to Lake Allatoona for something genuinely different.
scoring_notes: High uniqueness score because dragon boat racing is not something East Cobb residents see every day. Free to spectate removes friction. The charitable angle adds warmth. Slight audience-match deduction because it is a bit of a trek and the source does not confirm a 2026 date clearly, but the event is well-established and historically annual.

blurb: If you like browsing good art without fighting Midtown traffic, this one is worth the short drive up GA-400. The Alpharetta Arts Streetfest fills Wills Park with artisans, vendors, and street performers across two full days, plus activities for kids if yours are still around. It is the kind of event where you show up planning to stay an hour and leave two hours later with something for the wall. Free to get in, easy to park, and close enough that it does not feel like a field trip. Go Saturday if you want the energy; go Sunday if you want the elbow room.
scoring_notes: Alpharetta is a natural destination for East Cobb residents. The arts festival format fits the educated, higher-income audience well. Free admission removes any friction. Family-flexible: works for empty nesters browsing art and for families with teens. Annual event but only two days a year, so it has genuine can't-miss energy for the weekend.

date: Fridays, starting late May through summer
blurb: Forty years of Friday night concerts in Glover Park. That is a track record worth celebrating, and this summer's series is kicking off right in your backyard. Bring a blanket or a low chair, find a spot on the grass, and let the music do the rest. The opener this season is Yacht Rock Schooners, a '70s smooth rock group that is exactly as fun as it sounds. It is free, it is close, and it is one of those Marietta traditions that reminds you why you chose to live here. Make it a habit this summer.
scoring_notes: Marietta Square is in East Cobb's backyard. The 40th anniversary angle adds milestone significance to what is otherwise a recurring series. Yacht Rock Schooners is a pitch-perfect opener for the median-42-age crowd that grew up with that music. Free and low-effort, ideal for the empty nester and established family mix. Recurring series is the only uniqueness ding.
